structure_blocks.heading: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Determine the sourcing strategy When deciding on a sourcing strategy for procurement you should evaluate the following three key factors: method of supply; sourcing approach; and advertising strategy. Specifically, you should determine whether an existing method of supply should be used. Decide if the sourcing should be competitive or non-competitive and identify the most effective way to advertise. Public advertisement using the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS) is Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC)'s preferred notification process for competitive procurement. Buyers must confirm that funding is committed prior to the publication of any tender that results in a contract. Competitive The Government Contracts Regulations (GCRs) require the solicitation of offers before entering into any contract, though the GCRs provide for exceptions. Whenever possible, contractors must be selected using a competitive process. Your flexibility to depart from this approach and the type of competitive solicitation that may be used will depend on the procurement framework in place. Tendering approaches under the trade agreements There are three tendering approaches under trade agreements: open, selective, and limited. Each method has distinct procedural requirements to ensure transparency, fairness and compliance with the trade agreements. Open tendering This approach is characterized by you publishing a notice of proposed procurement (NPP) to invite all interested businesses to submit offers. Open tendering is the preferred approach. Selective tendering This approach involves either a one-time list or a permanent list of prequalified offerors. You must allow all prequalified offerors to participate in a procurement, unless the procuring entity specifies in its prequalification request any limitation on the number of offerors permitted to submit an offer and the criteria for that strategy. One-time list of prequalified offerors This is a two-stage process where you start by publishing an NPP to identify potential interested businesses who meet predetermined qualifications for participation. Then, you issue tender documentation to those prequalified offerors. For procurements subject to one or more international trade agreements, you must publish an NPP at both stages. It is also acceptable to amend the original NPP once the tender closing date has been determined. Permanent list of prequalified offerors This approach involves publishing an NPP to identify potential interested businesses that meet predetermined qualifications for participation. Next, you will develop a list of prequalified offerors, which will be maintained and used for several different individual solicitation processes. Tender documents are issued for individual solicitations under this list. Any other potential offeror who requests tender documentation must be considered, as long as there is sufficient time to carry out the prequalification process. You must publish an annual notice of the list's existence on the tender opportunities page, if covered by the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) or one or more international trade agreements. At PSPC , obligations regarding permanent lists of prequalified offerors are operationalized as “supply arrangements.” A permanent list of qualified offerors may also be referred to as a “source list” and is referred to in many trade agreements as a “multi-use list.” Limited tendering This approach allows you to move away from certain procedural obligations of the applicable trade agreement(s) under specific circumstances, for example, if you receive no suitable offers from open or selective tendering. You should not use limited tendering to avoid competition, to discriminate against any business, or to protect domestic businesses. It must comply with the trade agreements, particularly in technical specifications. Typically, limited tendering is competitive, but it can be non-competitive or used for sole sourcing when justified. You are advised to adhere closely to trade agreement obligations, deviating from procedural obligations only as necessary and permitted. For procurements covered by multiple trade agreements, the limited tendering provision of each agreement must contain a valid justification. It should also refer to the procedural obligations from which you may choose to deviate. In general, by complying with the obligations specified in the World Trade Organization – Agreement on Government Procurement (WTO-AGP) and Canada-European Union Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA), you will meet the requirements for the other trade agreements. Nonetheless, for clarity and compliance, referencing the specific limited tendering provisions in the applicable agreements is recommended. For examples of limited tendering provisions see: CFTA : Chapter 5, Article 513: Limited tendering CETA : Chapter 19, Article 19.12 Limited tendering WTO-AGP : Article XIII -Limited tendering Limited tendering reasons contained in the trade agreements Summary of limited tendering reasons Limited tendering can only be used in specific circumstances. If multiple trade agreements apply, you must include an appropriate limited tendering justification in the limited tendering provision of each trade agreement. All of Canada’s free trade agreements allow for limited tendering in these situations: If, in response to a prior notice, invitation to participate or invitation to tender: No tenders were submitted or no businesses requested to participate No tenders that conform to the essential requirements of the tender documentation were submitted No businesses met the conditions for participation The tenders submitted were collusive provided the procuring entity does not substantially modify the requirements in the notices or tender documentation. Where the goods or services can be supplied only by a particular business and no reasonable alternative or substitute goods or services exist for any of the following reasons : The requirement is for a work of art The protection of patents, copyrights, or other exclusive rights There is absence of competition for technical reasons For additional deliveries by the original contractor of goods or services that were not included in the initial procurement, where a change of contractor for such additional goods or services: Cannot occur for economic or technical reasons such as requirements of interchangeability or interoperability with existing equipment, software, services or installations procured under the initial procurement, and Would cause significant inconvenience or substantial duplication of costs for Canada Extreme urgency Only where strictly necessary, goods or services are needed urgently due to unforeseeable events and could not be obtained in time using open or selective tendering Commodity markets Goods are purchased on a commodity market Prototype procurement When procuring a prototype or first good or service that is developed at Canada’s request in the course of, and for, a particular contract for research, experiment, study or original development. Original development of a first good or service may include limited production or supply to incorporate the results of field testing and to demonstrate that the good or service is suitable for production or supply in a quantity to acceptable quality standards. It does not include quantity production or supply to establish commercial viability or to recover research and development costs. Subsequent procurements of these newly developed goods or services will be subject to the applicable agreement(s) Advantageous conditions Purchases are made under exceptionally advantageous conditions in the very short term in the case of unusual disposals such as from liquidation, receivership or bankruptcy, but not for routine purchases from regular suppliers Architectural design contest winners Where a contract is awarded to the winner of an architectural design contest, that has been organized in a manner that is consistent with the principles of the applicable agreements, in particular, relating to the publication of an NPP and where participants are judged by an independent jury with a view to a design contract being awarded to a winner Only the CFTA and Canada’s bilateral agreements with Chile, Colombia, Honduras, Panama, and Peru allow for limited tendering: Canadian Free Trade Agreement and Canada’s bilateral agreements with Chile, Colombia, Honduras, Panama and Peru When procuring for consulting services regarding confidential matters where disclosure could compromise government confidences or cause economic disruption, or similarly be contrary to the public interest Only the Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP) and Canada’s bilateral agreements with Chile, Honduras, and Panama allow for limited tendering: CPTPP and Canada’s bilateral agreements with Chile, Honduras and Panama If additional construction services that were not included in the initial contract but were within the objectives of the original tender documentation have, due to unforeseen cirmcumstances, become necessary to complete the construction services described therein. The total value of contracts awarded for additional construction services must not exceed 50% of the initial contract Only the CFTA allows for limited tendering: Canadian Free Trade Agreement If the goods or services can only be supplied by a particular business and no reasonable alternative or substitute goods or services exist for any of the following reasons: The supply is controlled by a supplier that is a statutory monopoly To ensure compatibility with existing goods or to maintain specialized goods that must be maintained by the manufacturer of those goods or its representative Work is to be performed on property by a contractor according to provisions of a warranty or guarantee held in respect of the property or the original work Work is to be performed on a leased building or related property or portions thereof, that can only be performed by the lessor The procurement is for subscriptions to newspapers, magazines or other periodicals If goods or consulting services regarding matters of a confidential or privileged nature are to be purchased and the disclosure of those matters through an open tendering process could reasonably be expected to compromise government confidentiality, result in the waiver of privilege, cause economic disruption, or otherwise be contrary to the public interest For the greatest certainty as to whether an appropriate limited tendering reason exists, buyers should refer to the limited tendering provisions found in the applicable trade agreements. Summary of procedural obligations that buyers may choose to deviate from when using limited tendering When a valid reason to use limited tendering exists, you may choose to move away from certain procedural obligations of the applicable trade agreements, such as: Notices (notices of intended procurement, summary notices, and notices of planned procurement) Conditions for participation Qualification of businesses (registration systems and qualification procedures, selective tendering, multi-use lists and information on procuring entity decisions) Tender documentation and modifications Time periods Treatment of tenders Awarding of contracts You must still follow the rules for technical specifications. When using limited tendering, it is recommended to follow trade agreement obligations as much as possible and only deviate as permitted and as needed. Refer to the limited tendering provisions in the applicable trade agreements to confirm the allowable procedural deviations. Crown corporations and additional notices Crown corporations may publish an NPP , typically at the beginning of the fiscal year, to advertise potential procurements. This NPP does not need responses from potential offerors but serves as an advanced indicator of future opportunities. Additionally, this NPP can be used to inform about a prequalification process. This NPP requires responses from offerors who wish to participate and meet the prequalification criteria. Tender documentation is issued to offerors that meet the criteria. Low dollar value Procurements that do not exceed $25,000 for goods or $40,000 for services and construction, inclusive of all taxes, are considered to be low dollar value (LDV) procurements. These procurements are carried out following the PSPC guidelines specified in the Directive on the Processing of Low Dollar Value Procurements (PDF, 27 KB, accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Note: You cannot split or divide requirements to fall within the LDV threshold. This ensures transparency and fairness in procurement processes. When selecting a contractor, you should use the most efficient and cost-effective method. This might involve soliciting offers, or directly selecting a sole offeror when soliciting offers is not cost-effective. You are tasked with choosing the most suitable procurement strategy for each LDV procurement. The aim is to obtain the best value while ensuring promptness and cost effectiveness in alignment with PSPC ’s guiding principles. Additionally, you must complete a risk assessment and document the procurement file. This must include the rationale behind the chosen procurement strategy and explain how the estimated contract value was determined. There are many tools available for identifying and selecting offerors, such as: Supplier Registration Information (SRI) system Tender opportunities page Telephone and online trade directories These tools support identifying and selecting offerors through competitive or directed means, enhancing efficiency and accessibility in procurement processes. Non-competitive When offers are not solicited, you must ensure that the client thoroughly justifies the use of any exception to the competitive solicitation. They need to cite the relevant exception under the Government Contracts Regulations (GCRs) of the Financial Administration Act (FAA) and considering the limited tendering provisions of Canada's trade agreements. You must also factor in the procurement provisions of the CLCA s. The GCRs generally require the solicitation of offers before entering a contract. A contract may be formed without soliciting offers when: The need is of pressing emergency that threatens public interest ( GCRs 6.(a)), such as actual or imminent life-threatening situations, disasters endangering quality of life or resulting in loss of life, or events causing significant damage to Crown property The estimated expenditure does not exceed: $25,000 for goods and $40,000 for services and construction contracts $100,000 for contracts involving architectural, engineering and other services related to the planning, design, preparation or supervision of construction, repair, renovation or restoration projects $100,000 for contracts entered into by the Minister of International Development involving architectural, engineering, or other services related to the planning, design, preparation or supervision of an international development assistance program or project ( GCRs 6.(b)) Soliciting offers would not serve the public interest due to the nature of the work ( GCRs 6.(c)) Only one entity is capable of performing the contract due to unique requirements, such as patents, copyrights, technical compatibility or specialised technological expertise ( GCRs 6.(d)) For procurements above $25,000 for goods and $40,000 for services and construction, greater diligence is required when invoking the exception where only one entity is capable of performing the work. In such cases, you must answer the TB questions for sole source , with support from the client. These responses should be included in the approval documents and procurement file. For procurements subject to trade agreements, you may award contracts without soliciting offers only if they align with one of the limited tendering reasons outlined in the applicable trade agreement. Employing limited tendering does not always mean a non-competitive process. Justification of non-competitive process Clients must clearly justify the rationale for any exception to soliciting offers. You are responsible for ensuring the rationale is adequately supported and you must also consider the procurement provisions under the CLCA s. If provided rationale is inadequate or if there is no supporting information, you are to guide the client towards alternative products or sources. The goal is to agree on the most suitable procurement strategy. If you are unable to reach a consensus with the client, escalate the matter to higher management. If you use any of the GCRs exceptions, you must include documentation detailing the procurement strategy and the reasoning for the selected exception. Additionally, when engaging in limited tendering under trade agreements, you must document the specific reasons and relevant articles justifying this approach. When issuing non-competitive standing offers, you must provide a rationale to justify using this process. If a standing offer is issued using a non-competitive procurement strategy or does not have a clear ranking methodology, resulting call-ups are considered non-competitive. In these situations, clients must provide the non-competitive rationale following the guidelines mentioned above. For example, if a non-competitive standing offer is issued to ABC Inc. with a sole source justification that the offeror has sole intellectual property (IP) rights for a unique scientific process, every subsequent call-up will require a distinct sole source justification. This justification must clearly demonstrate why ABC Inc.’s unique scientific process is the only acceptable method to meet the requirement and, if applicable, why other similar scientific processes would not be acceptable. Treasury Board questions for sole source To ensure the appropriateness of invoking exception 6.(d) from the GCRs for a sole source goods or services contract, the guidelines in the table below are provided to you with a detailed response process. This process is designed to thoroughly justify and explain the decision. You must consider all questions and prepare responses with the client. You must address each question and confirm non-applicability where necessary, to clearly outline the rationale behind the sole source selection. For contracts or standing offers for services, you are tasked with confirming that the procurement method chosen is the most suitable for the requirement. Methods include, but are not limited to, grants, contributions or various forms of employment contracts for example a term, a casual or ministerial appointment.. Sole source justification questions Number Question Client answer 1 Is the proposed sole source contract linked to a previous procurement and strategy for obtaining additional quantities and/or in-service support? If yes, what was the approved strategy? If "yes," specify the previous procurement strategy and file number for the previous contract (e.g. original procurement was posted "competitively" and it was identified that additional equipment would be purchased in the future with the successful vendor). Notwithstanding the approved strategy, is it feasible or affordable to compete the requirement? If "yes" to the first part, reassess if this requirement can be issued as a competitive requirement? If “no,” then answer the additional question below. If not, provide the related rationale in terms of cost, schedule, etc. If competing is not feasible, explain why, including factors like cost and schedule. 2 Does the offeror or its approved distributors have exclusive ownership of, and rights to use, the IP for the goods or services in question? If yes, provide details. What rights, if any, does the Crown have to use the IP ? If "yes," clarify if the offeror is the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) or the sole authorizer value-added reseller. Detail the justification for direct procurement from this offeror due to IP rights, for example: “We are dealing directly with the OEM , ABC Ltd, as they are the owners and developers of the equipment and IP , and they do not authorize value-added resellers or distributors.” “We are engaging XYZ Distribution Inc. as they are the sole distributor and approved value-added reseller for ABC Ltd.’s equipment in Canada.” 3 Are there legal or regulatory considerations precluding open competition for this good or service? If yes, provide details. Indicate any legislation or regulation mandating the selection the described offeror. 4 Are there alternative sources of supply for the same or equivalent materiel/support? If no, explain. Justify the lack of alternative sources, supporting the sole source rationale. If yes, what other options were considered and why were they not recommended? If "yes," alternate sources of supply exist, then explain their unsuitability. For example, indicate if research has been done to confirm unsuitability. Benchmarking outside a competitive process through PSPC is not allowed. 5 Is the proposal related to commonality/compatibility/with existing equipment? If yes, what are the operational costs/implications of managing multiple versions? If "yes," describe the need for compatibility and the consequences of multiple versions, including costs. At a minimum: clearly identify which equipment and or software that must be compatible with current requirement define what “compatibility” means to the client, be specific: do machines communicate between facilities? are samples compared across facilities? outline operational costs, and the implications of managing multiple versions and not maintaining compatibility 6 Explain why the price is fair and reasonable, describe how price support was obtained and summarize negotiations. To help your justification, the client can provide you with any preliminary financial quote received from the offeror. As the buyer responsible for negotiating the price, explain how you obtain a fair and reasonable price. 7 Are there any other factors that have led to a recommendation for a non-competitive process? If yes, provide details and rationale. Identify and provide the details of additional factors favoring a non-competitive approach. What is the likelihood of an amendment or follow-on contract to the same person? If the likelihood exists, explain your assessment for future procurement within the contract or follow-on contract with the same offeror. Describe the efforts taken to identify a variety of suppliers and explain any impact the Trade Agreement thresholds or TB Directive on the Management of Procurement contract entry/amendment limits will have on the proposed procurement strategy. Describe attempts to find alternative offerors and how trade agreements affect the strategy. Given the nature of your organization's mandate, describe any efforts taken to put in place long-term procurement arrangements to address similar requirements/activities in future (for example, establish standing offer). Describe the client efforts to identify any long-term procurement strategies to address future needs, for example by: exploring potential consolidation of opportunities with other departments. encouraging PSPC to include a broader range of equipment in any future standing offers whenever possible. Use a source list You should consider the following points when using a source list. Direct invitation Source lists primarily serve to request offers from offerors when a competitive procurement process isn't publicly advertised. When using source lists instead of rotational source lists, you can: Include any business that requests to participate and such businesses must be considered for evaluation Augment these lists with your knowledge of potential businesses and client recommendations, enriching the pool of candidates and potentially enhancing procurement outcomes Use a source list under the trade agreements For procurement requirements subject to the international trade agreements and the CFTA , source lists are useful for establishing a list of pre-qualified offerors of specific goods and services. They are managed through: Annual updates: You should refresh source lists at least annually, to maintain current and competitive offeror options Open tendering: You must use open tendering procedures using the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS) to invite businesses to submit their expressions of qualifications for evaluation and placement on the list, if they meet the selection criteria Businesses must be allowed to qualify at any time, and all who meet the selection criteria must be included on the pre-qualified list Specific considerations under the Canadian Free Trade Agreement For a solicitation of offers that is subject to the CFTA only, you must invite all pre-qualified offerors on the source list to submit offers. You must do this unless the pre-qualification invitation stated that there would be a limitation on the number of pre-qualified offerors allowed to submit offers. The pre-qualification invitation must also have included the criteria for selecting that limited number of pre-qualified offerors. In this case, you may limit the number of invited pre-qualified offerors, and you are not required to post an NPP on GETS . Compliance with international trade agreements Notice of proposed procurement (NPP): For solicitations that are subject to one or more international trade agreements you must publish an NPP on the Tender opportunities page. All pre-qualified offerors on the source list must be able to submit an offer. Inclusion of new businesses: You must allow businesses not initially on the source list to apply for pre-qualification upon request. Any businesses that qualify must be integrated into the source list in a timely manner and be allowed to participate in the procurement in question. Requirements not subject to trade agreements For procurement needs not subject to trade agreements, where open competition is not appropriate you should use source lists in the ways listed below. Use direct solicitation: You must solicit offers directly from a pre-qualified list of offerors for specific goods or services. If no such list exists, use the Supplier Registration Information service to identify potential offerors, particularly for low dollar value procurements. Include business recommendations: You may incorporate businesses recommended by the client into the source list to broaden potential sourcing options. Automated source lists You must use an automated source list system, such as the Automated Vendor Rotation System (AVRS), as applicable, to ensure systematic rotation of and equitable opportunities for all offerors. Opportunity to participate You must allow any offeror expressing interest on a specific requirement to submit an offer, except when it requires cancelling an existing solicitation of offers. This does not generally apply to rotational source lists, which may restrict participation to pre-qualified offerors. Best value and fairness You should make an effort to ensure best value to Canada in terms of who is invited. Ensure the principles of "fairness and access" are displayed in a practical manner by rotating opportunities to submit an offer within the offerors on any given list. Identification of and source lists for Indigenous businesses Use internal systems to identify Indigenous businesses who have self-declared as being Indigenous. These systems help compile comprehensive source lists of potential Indigenous businesses, applicable across all commodities—goods, services or construction. Solicitation process for Indigenous set-asides Always consider issuing a solicitation for a standing offer or a supply arrangement specifically for a Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business (PSIB) set-aside stream. This allows clients the possibility of contracting with Indigenous firms if they wish to do a set-aside procurement under PSIB , particularly for mandatory commodities. This approach enables clients to engage with Indigenous businesses under set-aside procurement initiatives. Additional resources for identifying Indigenous businesses Access the Indigenous Services Canada’s Indigenous Business Directory for more information on Indigenous businesses and to identify potential Indigenous businesses for procurement opportunities. If you have questions about the directory, contact the Business Development Directorate , Indigenous Services Canada (ISC), directly.

structure_blocks.heading: Crown corporations and additional notices
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Crown corporations may publish an NPP , typically at the beginning of the fiscal year, to advertise potential procurements. This NPP does not need responses from potential offerors but serves as an advanced indicator of future opportunities. Additionally, this NPP can be used to inform about a prequalification process. This NPP requires responses from offerors who wish to participate and meet the prequalification criteria. Tender documentation is issued to offerors that meet the criteria.

structure_blocks.heading: Low dollar value
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Low dollar value

structure_blocks.heading: Low dollar value
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Procurements that do not exceed $25,000 for goods or $40,000 for services and construction, inclusive of all taxes, are considered to be low dollar value (LDV) procurements. These procurements are carried out following the PSPC guidelines specified in the Directive on the Processing of Low Dollar Value Procurements (PDF, 27 KB, accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

structure_blocks.heading: Low dollar value
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Note: You cannot split or divide requirements to fall within the LDV threshold. This ensures transparency and fairness in procurement processes.

structure_blocks.heading: Low dollar value
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: When selecting a contractor, you should use the most efficient and cost-effective method. This might involve soliciting offers, or directly selecting a sole offeror when soliciting offers is not cost-effective. You are tasked with choosing the most suitable procurement strategy for each LDV procurement. The aim is to obtain the best value while ensuring promptness and cost effectiveness in alignment with PSPC ’s guiding principles.

structure_blocks.heading: Low dollar value
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Additionally, you must complete a risk assessment and document the procurement file. This must include the rationale behind the chosen procurement strategy and explain how the estimated contract value was determined.

structure_blocks.heading: Low dollar value
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: There are many tools available for identifying and selecting offerors, such as:

heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy

structure_blocks.heading: Low dollar value
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: These tools support identifying and selecting offerors through competitive or directed means, enhancing efficiency and accessibility in procurement processes.

heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: When offers are not solicited, you must ensure that the client thoroughly justifies the use of any exception to the competitive solicitation. They need to cite the relevant exception under the Government Contracts Regulations (GCRs) of the Financial Administration Act (FAA) and considering the limited tendering provisions of Canada's trade agreements. You must also factor in the procurement provisions of the CLCA s.

structure_blocks.heading: Non-competitive
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: The GCRs generally require the solicitation of offers before entering a contract.

structure_blocks.text: For procurements above $25,000 for goods and $40,000 for services and construction, greater diligence is required when invoking the exception where only one entity is capable of performing the work. In such cases, you must answer the TB questions for sole source , with support from the client. These responses should be included in the approval documents and procurement file.

structure_blocks.heading: Non-competitive
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: For procurements subject to trade agreements, you may award contracts without soliciting offers only if they align with one of the limited tendering reasons outlined in the applicable trade agreement. Employing limited tendering does not always mean a non-competitive process.

structure_blocks.heading: Justification of non-competitive process
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Justification of non-competitive process

structure_blocks.heading: Justification of non-competitive process
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Clients must clearly justify the rationale for any exception to soliciting offers. You are responsible for ensuring the rationale is adequately supported and you must also consider the procurement provisions under the CLCA s.

structure_blocks.heading: Justification of non-competitive process
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: If provided rationale is inadequate or if there is no supporting information, you are to guide the client towards alternative products or sources. The goal is to agree on the most suitable procurement strategy. If you are unable to reach a consensus with the client, escalate the matter to higher management.

structure_blocks.heading: Justification of non-competitive process
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: If you use any of the GCRs exceptions, you must include documentation detailing the procurement strategy and the reasoning for the selected exception. Additionally, when engaging in limited tendering under trade agreements, you must document the specific reasons and relevant articles justifying this approach.

structure_blocks.heading: Justification of non-competitive process
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: When issuing non-competitive standing offers, you must provide a rationale to justify using this process. If a standing offer is issued using a non-competitive procurement strategy or does not have a clear ranking methodology, resulting call-ups are considered non-competitive. In these situations, clients must provide the non-competitive rationale following the guidelines mentioned above.

structure_blocks.heading: Justification of non-competitive process
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: For example, if a non-competitive standing offer is issued to ABC Inc. with a sole source justification that the offeror has sole intellectual property (IP) rights for a unique scientific process, every subsequent call-up will require a distinct sole source justification. This justification must clearly demonstrate why ABC Inc.’s unique scientific process is the only acceptable method to meet the requirement and, if applicable, why other similar scientific processes would not be acceptable.

Treasury Board questions for sole source
structure_blocks.text: To ensure the appropriateness of invoking exception 6.(d) from the GCRs for a sole source goods or services contract, the guidelines in the table below are provided to you with a detailed response process. This process is designed to thoroughly justify and explain the decision. You must consider all questions and prepare responses with the client. You must address each question and confirm non-applicability where necessary, to clearly outline the rationale behind the sole source selection.

structure_blocks.heading: Treasury Board questions for sole source
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
Treasury Board questions for sole source
structure_blocks.text: For contracts or standing offers for services, you are tasked with confirming that the procurement method chosen is the most suitable for the requirement. Methods include, but are not limited to, grants, contributions or various forms of employment contracts for example a term, a casual or ministerial appointment..

structure_blocks.heading: Sole source justification questions

structure_blocks.text: Sole source justification questions

structure_blocks.heading: Sole source justification questions
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Determine the procurement strategy - Determine the sourcing strategy
structure_blocks.text: Number Question Client answer 1 Is the proposed sole source contract linked to a previous procurement and strategy for obtaining additional quantities and/or in-service support? If yes, what was the approved strategy? If "yes," specify the previous procurement strategy and file number for the previous contract (e.g. original procurement was posted "competitively" and it was identified that additional equipment would be purchased in the future with the successful vendor). Notwithstanding the approved strategy, is it feasible or affordable to compete the requirement? If "yes" to the first part, reassess if this requirement can be issued as a competitive requirement? If “no,” then answer the additional question below. If not, provide the related rationale in terms of cost, schedule, etc. If competing is not feasible, explain why, including factors like cost and schedule. 2 Does the offeror or its approved distributors have exclusive ownership of, and rights to use, the IP for the goods or services in question? If yes, provide details. What rights, if any, does the Crown have to use the IP ? If "yes," clarify if the offeror is the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) or the sole authorizer value-added reseller. Detail the justification for direct procurement from this offeror due to IP rights, for example: “We are dealing directly with the OEM , ABC Ltd, as they are the owners and developers of the equipment and IP , and they do not authorize value-added resellers or distributors.” “We are engaging XYZ Distribution Inc. as they are the sole distributor and approved value-added reseller for ABC Ltd.’s equipment in Canada.” 3 Are there legal or regulatory considerations precluding open competition for this good or service? If yes, provide details. Indicate any legislation or regulation mandating the selection the described offeror. 4 Are there alternative sources of supply for the same or equivalent materiel/support? If no, explain. Justify the lack of alternative sources, supporting the sole source rationale. If yes, what other options were considered and why were they not recommended? If "yes," alternate sources of supply exist, then explain their unsuitability. For example, indicate if research has been done to confirm unsuitability. Benchmarking outside a competitive process through PSPC is not allowed. 5 Is the proposal related to commonality/compatibility/with existing equipment? If yes, what are the operational costs/implications of managing multiple versions? If "yes," describe the need for compatibility and the consequences of multiple versions, including costs. At a minimum: clearly identify which equipment and or software that must be compatible with current requirement define what “compatibility” means to the client, be specific: do machines communicate between facilities? are samples compared across facilities? outline operational costs, and the implications of managing multiple versions and not maintaining compatibility 6 Explain why the price is fair and reasonable, describe how price support was obtained and summarize negotiations. To help your justification, the client can provide you with any preliminary financial quote received from the offeror. As the buyer responsible for negotiating the price, explain how you obtain a fair and reasonable price. 7 Are there any other factors that have led to a recommendation for a non-competitive process? If yes, provide details and rationale. Identify and provide the details of additional factors favoring a non-competitive approach. What is the likelihood of an amendment or follow-on contract to the same person? If the likelihood exists, explain your assessment for future procurement within the contract or follow-on contract with the same offeror. Describe the efforts taken to identify a variety of suppliers and explain any impact the Trade Agreement thresholds or TB Directive on the Management of Procurement contract entry/amendment limits will have on the proposed procurement strategy. Describe attempts to find alternative offerors and how trade agreements affect the strategy. Given the nature of your organization's mandate, describe any efforts taken to put in place long-term procurement arrangements to address similar requirements/activities in future (for example, establish standing offer). Describe the client efforts to identify any long-term procurement strategies to address future needs, for example by: exploring potential consolidation of opportunities with other departments. encouraging PSPC to include a broader range of equipment in any future standing offers whenever possible.

raw_text:
Determine the sourcing strategy When deciding on a sourcing strategy for procurement you should evaluate the following three key factors: method of supply; sourcing approach; and advertising strategy. Specifically, you should determine whether an existing method of supply should be used. Decide if the sourcing should be competitive or non-competitive and identify the most effective way to advertise. Public advertisement using the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS) is Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC)'s preferred notification process for competitive procurement. Buyers must confirm that funding is committed prior to the publication of any tender that results in a contract. Competitive The Government Contracts Regulations (GCRs) require the solicitation of offers before entering into any contract, though the GCRs provide for exceptions. Whenever possible, contractors must be selected using a competitive process. Your flexibility to depart from this approach and the type of competitive solicitation that may be used will depend on the procurement framework in place. Tendering approaches under the trade agreements There are three tendering approaches under trade agreements: open, selective, and limited. Each method has distinct procedural requirements to ensure transparency, fairness and compliance with the trade agreements. Open tendering This approach is characterized by you publishing a notice of proposed procurement (NPP) to invite all interested businesses to submit offers. Open tendering is the preferred approach. Selective tendering This approach involves either a one-time list or a permanent list of prequalified offerors. You must allow all prequalified offerors to participate in a procurement, unless the procuring entity specifies in its prequalification request any limitation on the number of offerors permitted to submit an offer and the criteria for that strategy. One-time list of prequalified offerors This is a two-stage process where you start by publishing an NPP to identify potential interested businesses who meet predetermined qualifications for participation. Then, you issue tender documentation to those prequalified offerors. For procurements subject to one or more international trade agreements, you must publish an NPP at both stages. It is also acceptable to amend the original NPP once the tender closing date has been determined. Permanent list of prequalified offerors This approach involves publishing an NPP to identify potential interested businesses that meet predetermined qualifications for participation. Next, you will develop a list of prequalified offerors, which will be maintained and used for several different individual solicitation processes. Tender documents are issued for individual solicitations under this list. Any other potential offeror who requests tender documentation must be considered, as long as there is sufficient time to carry out the prequalification process. You must publish an annual notice of the list's existence on the tender opportunities page, if covered by the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) or one or more international trade agreements. At PSPC , obligations regarding permanent lists of prequalified offerors are operationalized as “supply arrangements.” A permanent list of qualified offerors may also be referred to as a “source list” and is referred to in many trade agreements as a “multi-use list.” Limited tendering This approach allows you to move away from certain procedural obligations of the applicable trade agreement(s) under specific circumstances, for example, if you receive no suitable offers from open or selective tendering. You should not use limited tendering to avoid competition, to discriminate against any business, or to protect domestic businesses. It must comply with the trade agreements, particularly in technical specifications. Typically, limited tendering is competitive, but it can be non-competitive or used for sole sourcing when justified. You are advised to adhere closely to trade agreement obligations, deviating from procedural obligations only as necessary and permitted. For procurements covered by multiple trade agreements, the limited tendering provision of each agreement must contain a valid justification. It should also refer to the procedural obligations from which you may choose to deviate. In general, by complying with the obligations specified in the World Trade Organization – Agreement on Government Procurement (WTO-AGP) and Canada-European Union Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA), you will meet the requirements for the other trade agreements. Nonetheless, for clarity and compliance, referencing the specific limited tendering provisions in the applicable agreements is recommended. For examples of limited tendering provisions see: CFTA : Chapter 5, Article 513: Limited tendering CETA : Chapter 19, Article 19.12 Limited tendering WTO-AGP : Article XIII -Limited tendering Limited tendering reasons contained in the trade agreements Summary of limited tendering reasons Limited tendering can only be used in specific circumstances. If multiple trade agreements apply, you must include an appropriate limited tendering justification in the limited tendering provision of each trade agreement. All of Canada’s free trade agreements allow for limited tendering in these situations: If, in response to a prior notice, invitation to participate or invitation to tender: No tenders were submitted or no businesses requested to participate No tenders that conform to the essential requirements of the tender documentation were submitted No businesses met the conditions for participation The tenders submitted were collusive provided the procuring entity does not substantially modify the requirements in the notices or tender documentation. Where the goods or services can be supplied only by a particular business and no reasonable alternative or substitute goods or services exist for any of the following reasons : The requirement is for a work of art The protection of patents, copyrights, or other exclusive rights There is absence of competition for technical reasons For additional deliveries by the original contractor of goods or services that were not included in the initial procurement, where a change of contractor for such additional goods or services: Cannot occur for economic or technical reasons such as requirements of interchangeability or interoperability with existing equipment, software, services or installations procured under the initial procurement, and Would cause significant inconvenience or substantial duplication of costs for Canada Extreme urgency Only where strictly necessary, goods or services are needed urgently due to unforeseeable events and could not be obtained in time using open or selective tendering Commodity markets Goods are purchased on a commodity market Prototype procurement When procuring a prototype or first good or service that is developed at Canada’s request in the course of, and for, a particular contract for research, experiment, study or original development. Original development of a first good or service may include limited production or supply to incorporate the results of field testing and to demonstrate that the good or service is suitable for production or supply in a quantity to acceptable quality standards. It does not include quantity production or supply to establish commercial viability or to recover research and development costs. Subsequent procurements of these newly developed goods or services will be subject to the applicable agreement(s) Advantageous conditions Purchases are made under exceptionally advantageous conditions in the very short term in the case of unusual disposals such as from liquidation, receivership or bankruptcy, but not for routine purchases from regular suppliers Architectural design contest winners Where a contract is awarded to the winner of an architectural design contest, that has been organized in a manner that is consistent with the principles of the applicable agreements, in particular, relating to the publication of an NPP and where participants are judged by an independent jury with a view to a design contract being awarded to a winner Only the CFTA and Canada’s bilateral agreements with Chile, Colombia, Honduras, Panama, and Peru allow for limited tendering: Canadian Free Trade Agreement and Canada’s bilateral agreements with Chile, Colombia, Honduras, Panama and Peru When procuring for consulting services regarding confidential matters where disclosure could compromise government confidences or cause economic disruption, or similarly be contrary to the public interest Only the Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP) and Canada’s bilateral agreements with Chile, Honduras, and Panama allow for limited tendering: CPTPP and Canada’s bilateral agreements with Chile, Honduras and Panama If additional construction services that were not included in the initial contract but were within the objectives of the original tender documentation have, due to unforeseen cirmcumstances, become necessary to complete the construction services described therein. The total value of contracts awarded for additional construction services must not exceed 50% of the initial contract Only the CFTA allows for limited tendering: Canadian Free Trade Agreement If the goods or services can only be supplied by a particular business and no reasonable alternative or substitute goods or services exist for any of the following reasons: The supply is controlled by a supplier that is a statutory monopoly To ensure compatibility with existing goods or to maintain specialized goods that must be maintained by the manufacturer of those goods or its representative Work is to be performed on property by a contractor according to provisions of a warranty or guarantee held in respect of the property or the original work Work is to be performed on a leased building or related property or portions thereof, that can only be performed by the lessor The procurement is for subscriptions to newspapers, magazines or other periodicals If goods or consulting services regarding matters of a confidential or privileged nature are to be purchased and the disclosure of those matters through an open tendering process could reasonably be expected to compromise government confidentiality, result in the waiver of privilege, cause economic disruption, or otherwise be contrary to the public interest For the greatest certainty as to whether an appropriate limited tendering reason exists, buyers should refer to the limited tendering provisions found in the applicable trade agreements. Direct invitation Source lists primarily serve to request offers from offerors when a competitive procurement process isn't publicly advertised. When using source lists instead of rotational source lists, you can: Include any business that requests to participate and such businesses must be considered for evaluation Augment these lists with your knowledge of potential businesses and client recommendations, enriching the pool of candidates and potentially enhancing procurement outcomes Use a source list under the trade agreements For procurement requirements subject to the international trade agreements and the CFTA , source lists are useful for establishing a list of pre-qualified offerors of specific goods and services. You must do this unless the pre-qualification invitation stated that there would be a limitation on the number of pre-qualified offerors allowed to submit offers. The pre-qualification invitation must also have included the criteria for selecting that limited number of pre-qualified offerors. In this case, you may limit the number of invited pre-qualified offerors, and you are not required to post an NPP on GETS . Compliance with international trade agreements Notice of proposed procurement (NPP): For solicitations that are subject to one or more international trade agreements you must publish an NPP on the Tender opportunities page. All pre-qualified offerors on the source list must be able to submit an offer. Inclusion of new businesses: You must allow businesses not initially on the source list to apply for pre-qualification upon request. Any businesses that qualify must be integrated into the source list in a timely manner and be allowed to participate in the procurement in question. Requirements not subject to trade agreements For procurement needs not subject to trade agreements, where open competition is not appropriate you should use source lists in the ways listed below. Use direct solicitation: You must solicit offers directly from a pre-qualified list of offerors for specific goods or services. If no such list exists, use the Supplier Registration Information service to identify potential offerors, particularly for low dollar value procurements. Include business recommendations: You may incorporate businesses recommended by the client into the source list to broaden potential sourcing options. Automated source lists You must use an automated source list system, such as the Automated Vendor Rotation System (AVRS), as applicable, to ensure systematic rotation of and equitable opportunities for all offerors. Opportunity to participate You must allow any offeror expressing interest on a specific requirement to submit an offer, except when it requires cancelling an existing solicitation of offers. This does not generally apply to rotational source lists, which may restrict participation to pre-qualified offerors. Best value and fairness You should make an effort to ensure best value to Canada in terms of who is invited. Ensure the principles of "fairness and access" are displayed in a practical manner by rotating opportunities to submit an offer within the offerors on any given list. Identification of and source lists for Indigenous businesses Use internal systems to identify Indigenous businesses who have self-declared as being Indigenous. These systems help compile comprehensive source lists of potential Indigenous businesses, applicable across all commodities—goods, services or construction. This allows clients the possibility of contracting with Indigenous firms if they wish to do a set-aside procurement under PSIB , particularly for mandatory commodities. This approach enables clients to engage with Indigenous businesses under set-aside procurement initiatives. Determine the sourcing strategy

When deciding on a sourcing strategy for procurement you should evaluate the following three key factors: method of supply; sourcing approach; and advertising strategy.

Specifically, you should determine whether an existing method of supply should be used. Decide if the sourcing should be competitive or non-competitive and identify the most effective way to advertise.

Public advertisement using the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS) is Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC)'s preferred notification process for competitive procurement.

Buyers must confirm that funding is committed prior to the publication of any tender that results in a contract.

Competitive

The Government Contracts Regulations (GCRs) require the solicitation of offers before entering into any contract, though the GCRs provide for exceptions. Whenever possible, contractors must be selected using a competitive process. Your flexibility to depart from this approach and the type of competitive solicitation that may be used will depend on the procurement framework in place.

Tendering approaches under the trade agreements

There are three tendering approaches under trade agreements: open, selective, and limited. Each method has distinct procedural requirements to ensure transparency, fairness and compliance with the trade agreements.

Open tendering

This approach is characterized by you publishing a notice of proposed procurement (NPP) to invite all interested businesses to submit offers. Open tendering is the preferred approach.

Selective tendering

This approach involves either a one-time list or a permanent list of prequalified offerors. You must allow all prequalified offerors to participate in a procurement, unless the procuring entity specifies in its prequalification request any limitation on the number of offerors permitted to submit an offer and the criteria for that strategy.

This is a two-stage process where you start by publishing an NPP to identify potential interested businesses who meet predetermined qualifications for participation. Then, you issue tender documentation to those prequalified offerors. For procurements subject to one or more international trade agreements, you must publish an NPP at both stages. It is also acceptable to amend the original NPP once the tender closing date has been determined.

This approach involves publishing an NPP to identify potential interested businesses that meet predetermined qualifications for participation.

Next, you will develop a list of prequalified offerors, which will be maintained and used for several different individual solicitation processes. Tender documents are issued for individual solicitations under this list.

Any other potential offeror who requests tender documentation must be considered, as long as there is sufficient time to carry out the prequalification process.

You must publish an annual notice of the list's existence on the tender opportunities page, if covered by the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) or one or more international trade agreements.

At PSPC , obligations regarding permanent lists of prequalified offerors are operationalized as “supply arrangements.” A permanent list of qualified offerors may also be referred to as a “source list” and is referred to in many trade agreements as a “multi-use list.”

Limited tendering

This approach allows you to move away from certain procedural obligations of the applicable trade agreement(s) under specific circumstances, for example, if you receive no suitable offers from open or selective tendering.

You should not use limited tendering to avoid competition, to discriminate against any business, or to protect domestic businesses. It must comply with the trade agreements, particularly in technical specifications. Typically, limited tendering is competitive, but it can be non-competitive or used for sole sourcing when justified. You are advised to adhere closely to trade agreement obligations, deviating from procedural obligations only as necessary and permitted.

For procurements covered by multiple trade agreements, the limited tendering provision of each agreement must contain a valid justification. It should also refer to the procedural obligations from which you may choose to deviate.

In general, by complying with the obligations specified in the World Trade Organization – Agreement on Government Procurement (WTO-AGP) and Canada-European Union Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA), you will meet the requirements for the other trade agreements. Nonetheless, for clarity and compliance, referencing the specific limited tendering provisions in the applicable agreements is recommended.

For examples of limited tendering provisions see:

CFTA : Chapter 5, Article 513: Limited tendering

CETA : Chapter 19, Article 19.12 Limited tendering

WTO-AGP : Article XIII -Limited tendering

Additionally, when engaging in limited tendering under trade agreements, you must document the specific reasons and relevant articles justifying this approach.

When issuing non-competitive standing offers, you must provide a rationale to justify using this process. If a standing offer is issued using a non-competitive procurement strategy or does not have a clear ranking methodology, resulting call-ups are considered non-competitive. In these situations, clients must provide the non-competitive rationale following the guidelines mentioned above.

For example, if a non-competitive standing offer is issued to ABC Inc. with a sole source justification that the offeror has sole intellectual property (IP) rights for a unique scientific process, every subsequent call-up will require a distinct sole source justification. This justification must clearly demonstrate why ABC Inc.’s unique scientific process is the only acceptable method to meet the requirement and, if applicable, why other similar scientific processes would not be acceptable.

Treasury Board questions for sole source

To ensure the appropriateness of invoking exception 6.(d) from the GCRs for a sole source goods or services contract, the guidelines in the table below are provided to you with a detailed response process. This process is designed to thoroughly justify and explain the decision. You must consider all questions and prepare responses with the client. You must address each question and confirm non-applicability where necessary, to clearly outline the rationale behind the sole source selection.

For contracts or standing offers for services, you are tasked with confirming that the procurement method chosen is the most suitable for the requirement. Methods include, but are not limited to, grants, contributions or various forms of employment contracts for example a term, a casual or ministerial appointment..

Direct invitation

Augment these lists with your knowledge of potential businesses and client recommendations, enriching the pool of candidates and potentially enhancing procurement outcomes

Use a source list under the trade agreements

Compliance with international trade agreements

Inclusion of new businesses: You must allow businesses not initially on the source list to apply for pre-qualification upon request. Any businesses that qualify must be integrated into the source list in a timely manner and be allowed to participate in the procurement in question.

Requirements not subject to trade agreements

For procurement needs not subject to trade agreements, where open competition is not appropriate you should use source lists in the ways listed below.

Use direct solicitation: You must solicit offers directly from a pre-qualified list of offerors for specific goods or services. If no such list exists, use the Supplier Registration Information service to identify potential offerors, particularly for low dollar value procurements.

Include business recommendations: You may incorporate businesses recommended by the client into the source list to broaden potential sourcing options.

You must use an automated source list system, such as the Automated Vendor Rotation System (AVRS), as applicable, to ensure systematic rotation of and equitable opportunities for all offerors.

You must allow any offeror expressing interest on a specific requirement to submit an offer, except when it requires cancelling an existing solicitation of offers. This does not generally apply to rotational source lists, which may restrict participation to pre-qualified offerors.

You should make an effort to ensure best value to Canada in terms of who is invited. Ensure the principles of "fairness and access" are displayed in a practical manner by rotating opportunities to submit an offer within the offerors on any given list.

Identification of and source lists for Indigenous businesses

Use internal systems to identify Indigenous businesses who have self-declared as being Indigenous. These systems help compile comprehensive source lists of potential Indigenous businesses, applicable across all commodities—goods, services or construction.

Solicitation process for Indigenous set-asides

Always consider issuing a solicitation for a standing offer or a supply arrangement specifically for a Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business (PSIB) set-aside stream. This allows clients the possibility of contracting with Indigenous firms if they wish to do a set-aside procurement under PSIB , particularly for mandatory commodities. This approach enables clients to engage with Indigenous businesses under set-aside procurement initiatives.

Additional resources for identifying Indigenous businesses

Access the Indigenous Services Canada’s Indigenous Business Directory for more information on Indigenous businesses and to identify potential Indigenous businesses for procurement opportunities.

If you have questions about the directory, contact the Business Development Directorate , Indigenous Services Canada (ISC), directly.
